verdict_reasoning: STIM1 (Stromal Interaction Molecule 1) is a single-pass type I transmembrane protein resident in the ER membrane. It has a large N-terminal luminal domain (containing EF-hand Ca2+-sensing domains) and a cytoplasmic C-terminal domain. Crucially, a well-documented pool of STIM1 is also present at the plasma membrane, where it functions as a surface receptor component. Surface STIM1 has been detected by surface biotinylation, flow cytometry on intact cells, and is the target of cell-surface-directed antibody programs. At the PM, its N-terminal domain faces the extracellular space, making it accessible from outside. Beyond the ER-resident pool, the PM pool of STIM1 with an extracellular N-terminus is stable and constitutively present on many cell types, qualifying for a 'yes' verdict. It is not merely transient or condition-induced — basal PM STIM1 is detectable on resting cells and its extracellular domain is functionally relevant (e.g., interacts with extracellular fibronectin and other matrix proteins). Therefore, STIM1 qualifies as a classical single-pass surface protein with a constitutive PM pool.
predicted_key_uncertainty: STIM1 is predominantly ER-resident and only a minority pool is at the PM; whether the constitutive PM fraction is large enough to classify as 'yes' vs 'contextual/dual_localization' is debated in the literature.
